quiz.title: Unit 4 Quiz 12, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Advanced VSEPR Theory and Molecular Shapes
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: VSEPR Theory, Molecular Geometry, Electron Geometry, Lone Pair Effects, Regions of Electron Density, Exceptions and Complex Shapes

questions.question: What is the molecular shape of phosphorus trichloride (PCl₃)?
questions.explanation: PCl₃ has a trigonal pyramidal shape. Phosphorus has three bonding pairs and one lone pair, giving it tetrahedral electron geometry but trigonal pyramidal molecular shape.

questions.question: The bond angle in water (H₂O) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Water has a bond angle of approximately 104.5°. Although it has tetrahedral electron geometry (four regions of electron density: two bonding pairs and two lone pairs), the lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs, compressing the H–O–H bond angle from the ideal tetrahedral angle of 109.5° to about 104.5°.

questions.question: Ammonia (NH₃) has a bond angle of about:
questions.explanation: Ammonia (NH₃) has a bond angle of approximately 107°. It has tetrahedral electron geometry (four regions: three bonding pairs and one lone pair), but the lone pair repels more strongly than bonding pairs, compressing the H–N–H bond angles from the ideal 109.5° to about 107°.

questions.question: In a trigonal planar molecule like BF₃, the bond angles are:
questions.explanation: Trigonal planar molecules like BF₃ have bond angles of 120°. The three bonding pairs arrange themselves in a flat plane around the central atom, with 120° between each pair to minimize electron repulsion according to VSEPR theory.

questions.question: The bond angle in SF₆ (octahedral) is:
questions.explanation: Octahedral molecules like SF₆ have bond angles of 90° between adjacent bonds and 180° for opposite bonds. The question likely refers to the adjacent bond angle, which is 90°. The six bonding pairs arrange themselves pointing toward the vertices of an octahedron, with 90° angles between bonds in the same plane and to the axial positions.

questions.question: What is the bond angle in a linear molecule like CO₂?
questions.explanation: Linear molecules like CO₂ have a bond angle of 180°. The two regions of electron density around the central carbon atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible, resulting in a straight line geometry. This maximizes the distance between electron pairs and minimizes repulsion according to VSEPR theory.

questions.question: The bond angle in a tetrahedral molecule like CH₄ is:
questions.explanation: Tetrahedral molecules like CH₄ have bond angles of approximately 109.5°. This angle allows the four bonding pairs to be as far apart as possible in three-dimensional space, minimizing electron pair repulsion according to VSEPR theory.

questions.question: Which molecule has a trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry?
questions.explanation: PCl₅ has five bonding pairs and no lone pairs on phosphorus, giving it trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry with bond angles of 90°, 120°, and 180°.

questions.question: What is the shape of nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)?
questions.explanation: NO₂ has a bent shape. It has an odd number of electrons (free radical) with one unpaired electron, giving it a bond angle of about 134° (between linear and bent).

questions.question: Which molecule has a square planar shape?
questions.explanation: XeF₄ has a square planar shape. Xenon has four bonding pairs and two lone pairs, giving it octahedral electron geometry but square planar molecular shape.

questions.question: Bond shape in molecules is primarily determined by:
questions.explanation: According to VSEPR theory, molecular shapes are primarily determined by electron pair repulsion. Electron pairs (both bonding and lone pairs) arrange themselves to minimize repulsion.

questions.question: NO₂⁻ has how many regions of electron density?
questions.explanation: NO₂⁻ (nitrite ion) has three regions of electron density around nitrogen: two bonding regions (due to resonance, bonds are equivalent) and one lone pair.

questions.question: Electron geometry of HCN is:
questions.explanation: HCN has two regions of electron density around carbon (a triple bond to N counts as one region, and a single bond to H counts as one region), giving it linear electron geometry.

questions.question: CH₂O has electron geometry:
questions.explanation: CH₂O (formaldehyde) has three regions of electron density around carbon (two single bonds to H and one double bond to O), giving it trigonal planar electron geometry.

questions.question: Number of electron regions in SF₄ is:
questions.explanation: SF₄ has four bonding pairs and one lone pair on sulfur, giving it a total of five regions of electron density. This results in seesaw molecular shape with trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry.

questions.question: Electron geometry of ClF₃ is:
questions.explanation: ClF₃ has three bonding pairs and two lone pairs on chlorine, giving it five regions of electron density and trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry. Its molecular shape is T-shaped.

questions.question: CO₃²⁻ has electron geometry:
questions.explanation: CO₃²⁻ (carbonate ion) has three regions of electron density around carbon (due to resonance, all bonds are equivalent) and no lone pairs, giving it trigonal planar electron geometry.

questions.question: Electron geometry of ICl₄⁻ is:
questions.explanation: ICl₄⁻ has four bonding pairs and two lone pairs on iodine, giving it six regions of electron density and octahedral electron geometry. Its molecular shape is square planar.

questions.question: Number of electron regions in NH₄⁺ is:
questions.explanation: NH₄⁺ (ammonium ion) has four bonding pairs and no lone pairs on nitrogen, giving it four regions of electron density and tetrahedral geometry.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered advanced VSEPR theory and molecular shapes! 🧪📐
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review electron geometry vs molecular shape for molecules with lone pairs.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on counting electron regions correctly for complex molecules.
scoreMessages.0: Please review VSEPR theory, electron pair repulsion, and how to determine molecular shapes.
